If you are a publicly traded software company, and your customers access yo=
ur product through a server, and you provide them with a default password t=
o log into the server, and the default password is =E2=80=9Cpassword,=E2=80=
=9D is that securities fraud? You know the answer!=09Every bad thing that a=
 public company does is also securities fraud.=09Using =E2=80=9Cpassword=E2=
=80=9D as your password is pretty bad.=09Therefore, using =E2=80=9Cpassword=
=E2=80=9D as your password is securities fraud.Yesterday the  US Securities=
 and Exchange Commission sued =E2=80=9Csoftware company SolarWinds Corporat=
ion and its chief information security officer, Timothy G. Brown, for fraud=
 and internal control failures relating to allegedly known cybersecurity ri=
sks and vulnerabilities.=E2=80=9D SolarWinds sells network management softw=
are to companies and governments, including =E2=80=9Can information technol=
ogy infrastructure and management platform=E2=80=9D called Orion. In 2020, =
Orion was  famously hacked by Russian state actors, who inserted hidden cod=
e into Orion software updates and were =E2=80=9Cthen able to remotely explo=
it the networks and systems of SolarWinds=E2=80=99 customers,=E2=80=9D whic=
h they used =E2=80=9Cfor the primary purpose of espionage.=E2=80=9DThat was=
 bad for, among other things, SolarWinds=E2=80=99 stock price. The  SEC=E2=
=80=99s complaint says:On December 14, 2020, the day it filed the Form 8-K =
first announcing the SUNBURST attack against the Orion platform, SolarWinds=
=E2=80=99 stock price dropped more than 16%. It dropped at least an additio=
nal 8% the next day. The stock price continued to drop and lost approximate=
ly 35% of its value by the end of the month as SolarWinds disclosed more de=
tails of the SUNBURST attack, and as news outlets reported that internal so=
urces had warned SolarWinds for several years about the Company=E2=80=99s c=
ybersecurity risks and vulnerabilities.My non-technical theory is that =E2=
=80=9Ceverything is securities fraud=E2=80=9D: If a public company does a b=
ad thing, or a bad thing happens to it, and the stock drops, then that is s=
ecurities fraud. The stock=C2=A0was=C2=A0high, because investors did not kn=
ow about the company=E2=80=99s vulnerability to the bad thing. Then the bad=
 thing happened, investors found out, and the stock dropped. Before they fo=
und out, they were deceived about the true value of the company, so that wa=
s fraud. Here, that is all you really need to know: bad thing, stock down, =
securities fraud.But that is not technically an accurate description of the=
 law, so the SEC, in suing SolarWinds, needs to argue that SolarWinds made =
false statements about facts that were material to investors. Thus, for exa=
mple, =E2=80=9Cpassword=E2=80=9D:SolarWinds=E2=80=99 Security Statement fal=
sely claimed the Company not only had, but enforced, a strong password poli=
cy. Specifically, SolarWinds and Brown stated: =E2=80=9CWe require that aut=
horized users be provisioned with unique account IDs. Our password policy c=
overs all applicable information systems, applications, and databases. Our =
password best practices enforce the use of complex passwords that include b=
oth alpha and numeric characters, which are deployed to protect against una=
uthorized use of passwords.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6Contrary to its Security Stat=
ement, SolarWinds did not enforce strong password requirements on all of it=
s information systems, applications, and databases, as Brown and SolarWinds=
 knew or were reckless or negligent in not knowing. =E2=80=A6In an April 20=
17 email to the newly hired CIO, a SolarWinds employee expressed surprise t=
hat things =E2=80=9Clike =E2=80=98default passwords=E2=80=99 are [still] pl=
aguing us when the product has been in the market [this long,]=E2=80=9D exp=
laining, =E2=80=9C[m]any of these vulnerabilities seem pretty well amateur =
hour.=E2=80=9D As an example, the employee noted one product for which the =
default password was =E2=80=9Cpassword.=E2=80=9D Senior InfoSec Manager E t=
estified that having a default password of =E2=80=9Cpassword=E2=80=9D is a =
=E2=80=9Cpoor security practice.=E2=80=9DAnd possibly even better, =E2=80=
=9Csolarwinds123=E2=80=9D:During the Relevant Period, SolarWinds used an Ak=
amai server to distribute software updates to its customers. In November 20=
19, an outside security researcher notified SolarWinds that the password fo=
r the Company=E2=80=99s Akamai server was publicly available, and that a th=
reat actor could use that public password to infect SolarWinds=E2=80=99 sof=
tware updates: =E2=80=9CI have found a public Github repo which is leaking =
ftp credential belong[ing] to SolarWinds=E2=80=A6. Via this any hacker coul=
d upload malicious exe [executable code] and update it with release [of] So=
larWinds product.=E2=80=9D Senior InfoSec Manager E confirmed the security =
researcher=E2=80=99s description. The password that was publicly available =
was =E2=80=9Csolarwinds123,=E2=80=9D an astonishingly simple password that =
did not comply with the Company=E2=80=99s stated password complexity requir=
ements.=E2=80=9CAny hacker could upload malicious exe=E2=80=9D is pretty mu=
ch what ultimately happened and crashed the stock. I am not a cybersecurity=
 expert and I have not followed the SolarWinds hack closely, but I suppose =
it is possible that Russian intelligence agents were able to hack the Orion=
 updates, and thus spy on US federal government computers, by correctly gue=
ssing the password =E2=80=9Csolarwinds123.=E2=80=9D What would  Dark Helmet=
 say?But that=E2=80=99s not the point; the point is that SolarWinds=C2=A0in=
vestors=C2=A0were, let us just hypothesize, closely reading the company=E2=
=80=99s =E2=80=9CSecurity Statement,=E2=80=9D a document that was not actua=
lly included in SolarWinds=E2=80=99 securities filings or financial reports=
 but that was =E2=80=9Cposted to the Company=E2=80=99s website.=E2=80=9D An=
d, says the SEC, they were deceived:SolarWinds and Brown=E2=80=99s misstate=
ments and omissions regarding password issues were not only false and misle=
ading, but materially so. A reasonable investor, considering whether to pur=
chase or sell SolarWinds stock, would have considered it important to know =
the true state of SolarWinds=E2=80=99 password policies, especially conside=
ring that these issues were longstanding and potentially affected customer-=
facing areas such as the Akamai server used to send updates to customers.Is=
 that right? It feels not quite right, in the sense that you rarely see equ=
ity research notes about public companies that are like =E2=80=9Cupgrading =
this company to a Buy based on its strong password policies.=E2=80=9D The c=
laim here is not=C2=A0really, not=C2=A0seriously, that investors read Solar=
Winds=E2=80=99=C2=A0password policy, and decided to invest based on that po=
licy, and then lost money when the password policy turned out to be fake. T=
he more likely story is that investors blithely assumed most companies have=
 good practices across a range of domains and figured that, if SolarWinds r=
eally=C2=A0was=C2=A0just letting anyone into its software, someone would te=
ll them.=C2=A0It=E2=80=99s not just passwords, to be clear. The SEC identif=
ies other kinds of poor security information security practices; the =E2=80=
=9Cpassword=E2=80=9D stuff is just the most obvious. The basic idea is that=
 SolarWinds did various careless things, while telling customers and invest=
ors that it was careful, and eventually the carelessness caught up to it. A=
nd the stock dropped.What is the lesson that companies should take from thi=
s case? The SEC complaint says that, even as SolarWinds was publicly saying=
 that it had a good security policy, =E2=80=9CBrown and other SolarWinds em=
ployees knew that SolarWinds had serious cybersecurity deficiencies.=E2=80=
=9D It cites a bunch of internal emails and messages:An August 2019 present=
ation warned that =E2=80=9C[a]ccess and privilege to critical systems / dat=
a is inappropriate.=E2=80=9DPresentations in March and October 2020 highlig=
hted =E2=80=9C[s]ignificant deficiencies=E2=80=9D in SolarWinds=E2=80=99 ac=
cess controls. =E2=80=A6In a July 2020 email to Brown, a member of the Engi=
neering team described being =E2=80=9Cspooked=E2=80=9D by activity at a Sol=
arWinds=E2=80=99 customer. Brown agreed that the incident was =E2=80=9Cvery=
 concerning=E2=80=9D and continued, =E2=80=9CAs you guys know our backends =
are not that resilient and we should definitely make them better.=E2=80=9DA=
 September 2020 Risk Acceptance Form flagged for Brown and others =E2=80=9C=
the risk of legacy issues in the Orion Platform=E2=80=9D and warned =E2=80=
=9C[t]he volume of security issues being identified over the last month hav=
e outstripped the capacity of Engineering teams to resolve.=E2=80=9DIn inst=
ant messages sent in November 2020, SolarWinds=E2=80=99 Senior InfoSec Mana=
ger E expressed his own disgust with the Company=E2=80=99s security posture=
, lamenting, =E2=80=9C[W]e=E2=80=99re so far from being a security minded c=
ompany. [E]very time I hear about our head geeks talking about security I w=
ant to throw up.=E2=80=9DIn November 2020, a SolarWinds Information Securit=
y employee sent an instant message to Senior InfoSec Manager E with a link =
to a list of vulnerabilities in the Orion platform stating, =E2=80=9CThe pr=
oducts are riddled and obviously have been for many years.=E2=80=9DIf you r=
un information security at a software company, would you rather get message=
s like this, or not? Like:=09Ideally your security would be perfect and you=
 would never get messages like this because there are no problems.=09If you=
r security is not perfect, wouldn=E2=80=99t you=C2=A0rather get messages li=
ke this than not?? For one thing, strong clear intemperate statements like =
this will alert you to the problem better than diplomatic wishy-washy messa=
ges. For another thing, you might want to employ engineers who send message=
s like this: You want your employees to be morally indignant at security vu=
lnerabilities, to get mad about them, so they are likely to catch and fix t=
hem. You want people who are passionate about security, and if you find the=
m you will tolerate their negativity and complaining.But if you run the leg=
al department at a software company, these messages are really bad! They le=
ad pretty directly to securities fraud charges. I   once wrote:If you are t=
rying to build a good engineering culture, you might want to encourage your=
 employees to send hyperbolic, overstated, highly quotable emails to a broa=
d internal distribution list when they object to a decision. On the other h=
and your lawyers, and your public relations people, will obviously and corr=
ectly tell you that that is insane.Everything is securities fraud, and usin=
g =E2=80=9Cpassword=E2=80=9D as your password is securities fraud, but usin=
g =E2=80=9Cpassword=E2=80=9D as your password is especially securities frau=
d if one of your engineers sends an email saying that it is =E2=80=9Cpretty=
 well amateur hour.=E2=80=9D But it is pretty well amateur hour! It is help=
ful for someone to point that out. But it gets you sued.

      Bed Bath from the Beyond
   =20
 =20
When did the shares of Bed Bath & Beyond Inc.=E2=80=99s stock become worthl=
ess? I think a reasonable answer would be January 2023: Bed Bath & Beyond b=
reached its debt covenants in December 2022, hired financial advisers to fi=
nd a potential buyer (in bankruptcy), and came up empty. By January, it was=
 planning to file for bankruptcy and liquidate its assets, which would brin=
g in some money for creditors, but not enough to pay them in full, and cert=
ainly not with anything left over for shareholders.But Bed Bath & Beyond fo=
und a way to delay the inevitable: It had enthusiastic retail meme-stock in=
vestors, and it did a series of  weird deals to sell them an absolute ton o=
f stock, for ever-declining prices, to raise a bit more money to hand over =
to its creditors. This was pretty clearly the plan, and it was pretty clear=
ly disclosed; Bed Bath was not tricking=C2=A0shareholders about what it was=
 doing. But it did it anyway, and the shareholders happily tricked themselv=
es, pouring money into a worthless company for it to hand over to creditors=
. This went on for a few months, and then Bed Bath & Beyond did file for ba=
nkruptcy in April. I  wrote at the time:This is the peak of meme stocks. Be=
d Bath & Beyond sold 50 million shares a week for three months with, as far=
 as I can tell, no story, no plan, nothing but =E2=80=9Ca troubled financia=
l situation and nostalgia value.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0Bed Bath saw that its retail=
 shareholders wanted to throw their money away, and that its sophisticated =
lenders wanted to get their money back, and realized that there was a trade=
 to be done that would make everyone, temporarily, happy. So it did the tra=
de.But why stop there? By April,=C2=A0Bed Bath & Beyond shareholders had be=
en buying worthless stock, for meme and confusion reasons, for months; why =
would bankruptcy stop them? And so in fact investors  spent hundreds of mil=
lions of dollars trading Bed Bath & Beyond stock=C2=A0after=C2=A0it filed f=
or bankruptcy, possibly out of pure confusion, though possibly out of optim=
ism that there would end up being some recovery for shareholders. That is n=
ot a theoretical impossibility =E2=80=94=C2=A0notably shareholders got paid=
 in  the Hertz Global Holdings Inc. bankruptcy =E2=80=94=C2=A0but it was va=
nishingly unlikely, and it did not in fact happen. On Sept. 29, 2023, Bed B=
ath & Beyond exited bankruptcy with an approved plan that resulted in zero =
recovery for shareholders. It  said in a securities filing:As a result of t=
he Confirmed Plan becoming effective, all of the Company=E2=80=99s equity i=
nterests, consisting of outstanding shares of common stock and Series A Con=
vertible Preferred Stock of the Company and related rights to receive or pu=
rchase shares of common stock, were cancelled on the Effective Date without=
 consideration and have no value.=C2=A0That same day, it  terminated the re=
gistration of its stock, which, I hope, stopped it from trading. The last p=
rice that I see for Bed Bath & Beyond stock on Bloomberg was on Sept. 29, w=
ith a closing price of $0.0789 per share,=C2=A08.8 million shares traded an=
d a market capitalization of  $62 million.But why stop there? Fidelity Inve=
stments, the retail broker,  removed Bed Bath & Beyond shares from its cust=
omers=E2=80=99 accounts on Oct. 18, because those shares really were cancel=
ed. (Presumably other brokers removed them too.)=C2=A0And you can go to Fid=
elity=E2=80=99s Twitter account and  find people=C2=A0complaining. =E2=80=
=9CA stock broker like #Fidelity should not remove #BBBYQ shares from holde=
r's accounts nor their P&L statement if there's a possibility of restructur=
ing or corporate action in the near-term future.=E2=80=9D (There is not.)  =
And: =E2=80=9CMine have disappeared=E2=80=A6 @Fidelity Replace my shares, p=
lease.=E2=80=9D  And: =E2=80=9CWhat do you mean expired? How can shares hel=
d in cash account expire?=E2=80=9DSome of these people are probably kidding=
. I suspect some aren=E2=80=99t, though. Presumably the people who paid act=
ual money to buy Bed Bath & Beyond shares in September, or June, or even Fe=
bruary, were not kidding, but it didn=E2=80=99t make much more sense then.P=
ossibly the meme stock craze was =E2=80=A6=C2=A0bad? The run-up in meme sto=
cks in 2021 might have over-taught the lesson that financial markets are a =
random casino, that anyone can make a lot of money for no reason, and that =
if for some reason you=C2=A0lose=C2=A0money it=E2=80=99s probably the fault=
 of evil hedge funds protecting their power. And so now you can buy stock i=
n a bankrupt company, and the company can cancel its stock because it doesn=
=E2=80=99t have enough money to pay off creditors, and you can get mad abou=
t it.

      SBF Stuff
   =20
 =20
I   wrote yesterday that the reviews of Sam Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s testimo=
ny at his trial, on direct examination, were basically pretty good. But I f=
igured the reviews of his performance on cross-examination would be less go=
od. They were less good. =E2=80=9CIt's not going well,=E2=80=9D  writes Dan=
 Primack. =E2=80=9CPartially because SBF keeps trying to sound like the sma=
rtest guy in the room, and partially because he seems to believe the entire=
 proceeding is beneath him.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9CDuring an approximately four-=
hour cross-examination led by prosecutor Danielle Sassoon, Bankman-Fried ap=
peared to suffer from memory loss, evaded questions, and gave downright unh=
elpful answers,=E2=80=9D  writes Tracy Wang. =E2=80=9CWhen confronted with =
a difficult question, Bankman-Fried would often rely on his vast vocabulary=
 of weasel phrases.=E2=80=9D Here=E2=80=99s  Bloomberg News:Bankman-Fried w=
as asked whether he fired anyone for spending $8 billion of customer funds.=
 =E2=80=9CNo,=E2=80=9D he said. When asked how the money was spent -- exclu=
sively for margin trading or not -- Bankman-Fried said, =E2=80=9CI am not s=
ure.=E2=80=9D He evaded other questions, such as whether he would agree he =
wasn=E2=80=99t transparent with customers about risks posed by FTX. =E2=80=
=9CIt wasn=E2=80=99t our policy to disclose customer account details,=E2=80=
=9D he replied. ...=E2=80=9CI am not sure=E2=80=9D made more than one appea=
rance, with Bankman-Fried often not replying directly to questions. The pro=
secutor and judge at times had to ask the same question over and over to ge=
t an answer. Bankman-Fried also frequently repeated that he didn=E2=80=99t =
remember what he had seen or said.And here=E2=80=99s  the Wall Street Journ=
al:The trial proceedings shifted quickly after Assistant U.S. Attorney Dani=
elle Sassoon began her cross-examination and sought to confront Bankman-Fri=
ed with a litany of his past public statements whose truthfulness she quest=
ioned.=E2=80=9CWould you agree that you know how to tell a good story?=E2=
=80=9D Sassoon asked.=E2=80=9CI don=E2=80=99t know. It depends on what metr=
ic you use,=E2=80=9D Bankman-Fried responded.=C2=A0Correct? Like if you jud=
ge Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s ability to tell a story by its=C2=A0maximum=C2=
=A0payoff at any point in time, he was better at telling stories than almos=
t anyone else in human history? If you judge it by its=C2=A0final=C2=A0payo=
ff, quite bad. Similarly:The prosecutor appeared to be goading Bankman-Frie=
d at certain moments, pointing out FTX=E2=80=99s meteoric rise, his onetime=
 status as a billionaire and his college degree from the Massachusetts Inst=
itute of Technology.=C2=A0=E2=80=9CYou think of yourself as a smart guy?=E2=
=80=9D she asked.=E2=80=9CIn many ways. Not in all ways,=E2=80=9D he replie=
d.=C2=A0Same basic analysis. And here is  the Financial Times:He admitted t=
o calling a =E2=80=9Cspecific subset=E2=80=9D of crypto investors =E2=80=9C=
dumb [expletives]=E2=80=9D and conceded he had referred to his advocacy for=
 crypto regulation as =E2=80=9Cjust PR=E2=80=9D in the days leading up to h=
is arrest last December.He also admitted to writing =E2=80=9C[expletive]=C2=
=A0regulators=E2=80=9D to a reporter in November 2022.If you call your inve=
stors =E2=80=9Cdumb [expletives],=E2=80=9D does that prove that you are doi=
ng fraud on them? No. Is it a good look, at your fraud trial? No. Is it hel=
pful to clarify that you were only referring to a =E2=80=9Cspecific subset=
=E2=80=9D of your investors that way? Probably not that much?Elsewhere, her=
e is a  profile of Tiffany Fong, the =E2=80=9Creluctant crypto content crea=
tor=E2=80=9D who spent a lot of time talking to Bankman-Fried while he was =
on house arrest:Fong and Bankman-Fried reconnected in November 2022, when h=
is FTX empire swiftly and dramatically imploded. On November 11, the day FT=
X filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy, Fong reached out to Bankman-Fried. =E2=
=80=9CI just said something like, =E2=80=98Hey, obviously, there=E2=80=99s =
been a lot of news about FTX. Would you be willing to chat with me and tell=
 me your side of the story?=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D recalls Fong. =E2=80=9CI real=
ly did not think that Sam would ever get back to me.=E2=80=9DA few days lat=
er, Fong was on a late-night date at a Brooklyn dive bar when Bankman-Fried=
 responded. He sent her a heart emoji and said he=E2=80=99d be free to chat=
 with her for the next hour or so. =E2=80=9CI was like, =E2=80=98What? No, =
there=E2=80=99s no [expletive] way=E2=80=99,=E2=80=9D says Fong. She said t=
o her date that evening, =E2=80=9CI don=E2=80=99t even know what to ask him=
. I=E2=80=99m kind of drunk right now.=E2=80=9D Her date, who she=E2=80=99d=
 met through crypto and had substantial funds stuck on FTX, offered, =E2=80=
=9CAsk him where my [expletive] money is.=E2=80=9DStill elsewhere, Bloomber=
g=E2=80=99s   Emily Nicolle reports:As the trial of FTX co-founder Sam Bank=
man-Fried unfolds, the relationship between his exchange=E2=80=99s trading =
outfit Alameda Research and stablecoin issuer Tether is under the microscop=
e of crypto sleuths.Alameda was Tether=E2=80=99s largest non-exchange custo=
mer between 2020 and 2022, with blockchain data showing it received almost =
$40 billion in transfers of its stablecoin USDT directly from the company =
=E2=80=94 equal to roughly 20% of all USDT tokens ever issued.The vast amou=
nt has raised questions about where Alameda got the money to fund the issua=
nce of the stablecoin, which is typically used as a dollar proxy and is the=
 world=E2=80=99s most traded cryptocurrency. That even led to speculation A=
lameda might have used other means to help fund the purchases, similar to i=
ts bets on startups or dealings with lenders.I feel like eventually Tether =
is going to be an incredibly interesting story, but I still don=E2=80=99t k=
now what it is.

      Ares
   =20
 =20
Two fundamental questions of bank regulation are:=09Would you rather have s=
ome class of loans made by=C2=A0banks, which are subject to comprehensive r=
egulation and supervision, or by relatively less regulated non-banks?=09Wou=
ld you rather have some class of loans funded by=C2=A0demand deposits, whic=
h could flee at any time, or by pension funds, which can=E2=80=99t?These qu=
estions are largely the same question. Here=E2=80=99s Bloomberg=E2=80=99s=
=C2=A0Allison McNeely:Ares Management Corp. raised $6.6 billion for its sec=
ond asset-based credit fund, as it seeks to snap up portfolios from banks t=
hat want to sell them to comply with higher capital requirements.The firm=
=E2=80=99s Pathfinder Fund II, which exceeded its $5 billion target, will i=
nvest in portfolios of assets that offer steady cash flows, Ares said in a =
statement Monday. That could include pools of auto loans, credit card recei=
vables, mortgages and other bundles of loans. =E2=80=A6Ares has been active=
ly negotiating with banks that aim to offload loan portfolios that have hig=
h capital charges, which makes them less profitable to hold on their balanc=
e sheets.It recently closed on a so-called capital relief trade for a portf=
olio of more than $5 billion of =E2=80=9Csuper-prime=E2=80=9D auto loans fr=
om a large regional bank, according to Joel Holsinger, co-head of alternati=
ve credit at Ares. He predicts a rush of deals before year-end.=E2=80=9CThi=
s is no different than where direct lending was in 2011 or 2012,=E2=80=9D H=
olsinger said in an interview. He added that a =E2=80=9Cfragmentation=E2=80=
=9D ensued after the 2008 financial crisis and that =E2=80=9Cexcessive regu=
lation=E2=80=9D has pushed certain asset classes out of banks. =E2=80=A6For=
 institutional investors, asset-based credit offers diversity and uncorrela=
ted risk beyond traditional direct loans to companies, Keith Ashton, co-hea=
d of alternative credit at Ares, said in an interview. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0In fi=
ve years, most pensions will allocate 2% to 4% of their portfolios toward a=
sset-based credit, Holsinger said.Is this good? On the one hand it is not h=
ard to find people complaining about the rise of private credit and arguing=
 that firms like Ares are less regulated, and more willing to take risks, t=
han banks are. On the other hand, banks really are   funded by deposits! Th=
at has caused problems this year: If you borrow short-term to lend long-ter=
m, you can   run into trouble. Whereas if you raise money from pension fund=
s to buy auto loans, you really can hold those auto loans to maturity.=C2=
=A0

      Coins
   =20
 =20
We  talked the other day about some criminals who stole 2 million dimes and=
 were like =E2=80=9Cugh, now we have to convert these into money.=E2=80=9D =
The conversion was terribly labor-intensive and also got them caught. If yo=
u stand at the CoinStar machine all day pouring in dimes, people will have =
questions. Coins, in the US, are money, but only in pretty small amounts. O=
nce you get above roughly a pocketful of coins, their moneyness starts to f=
all off.A forklift-full of coins, for example,=C2=A0is somehow the opposite=
 of money:Pennies, nickels, dimes and quarters might be legal tender but mo=
re than 6,500 pounds of loose change is not a proper form of payment, a Col=
orado judge ruled last week after a defendant attempted to deliver $23,500 =
in coins to settle a legal dispute.The judge, Joseph Findley, of Larimer Co=
unty, said that the delivery of more than three tons was done =E2=80=9Cmali=
ciously and in bad faith,=E2=80=9D and that the defendant, a welding compan=
y, must now pay more for its act. ...JMF Enterprises attempted to make a =
=E2=80=9Cnighttime delivery=E2=80=9D to Fired Up Fabrications but company o=
fficials rejected it because they at first thought it was a forklift being =
delivered, according to the judge=E2=80=99s order.On Aug. 28, the following=
 Monday, =E2=80=9Can attempt was made to deliver a heavy metal container of=
 coins that required a forklift to move=E2=80=9D to lawyers for Fired Up Fa=
brications, the order said, but it was =E2=80=9Cphysically impossible=E2=80=
=9D to deliver. =E2=80=A6In the order, Judge Findley said that while coins =
were legal tender, paying such a large settlement in coins would reduce the=
 settlement because of the time and expense required to accept it.He said p=
hotographs showed that the coins had also been removed from neatly organize=
d boxes and dumped =E2=80=9Cloosely and randomly=E2=80=9D into a metal cont=
ainer.The judge ordered JMF Enterprises and Mr. Frank to pay additional fee=
s related to the costs of extending the case and dealing with the coin paym=
ent.Surely everyone who has lost a lawsuit has briefly fantasized about pay=
ing in pennies. Surely Elon Musk had someone look into paying $44 billion f=
or Twitter in pennies. But here=E2=80=99s some legal precedent. If you try =
to pay someone with 6,500 pounds of coins, (1) you will have to pay them ag=
ain with real money, (2) you will have to pay them=C2=A0more=C2=A0and (3) y=
ou=E2=80=99ll be stuck with all the coins.

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">SolarWinds</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you are a publicly traded software =
company, and your customers access your product through a server, and you p=
rovide them with a default password to log into the server, and the default=
 password is =E2=80=9Cpassword,=E2=80=9D is that securities fraud? You know=
 the answer!</p><ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; =
margin: 16px 0;">=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Every bad thing that =
a public company does is also securities fraud.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-=
bottom: 5px;">Using =E2=80=9Cpassword=E2=80=9D as your password is pretty b=
ad.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Therefore, using =E2=80=9Cpass=
word=E2=80=9D as your password is securities fraud.</li></ol><p style=3D"ma=
rgin: 16px 0;">Yesterday the <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness=
.com/click/33208705.258413/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9uZXdzL3ByZXNzLXJlbGVh=
c2UvMjAyMy0yMjc/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB2419b46c" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" i=
temscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-deco=
ration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #00=
0000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> US Securities a=
nd Exchange Commission sued</a> =E2=80=9Csoftware company SolarWinds Corpor=
ation and its chief information security officer, Timothy G. Brown, for fra=
ud and internal control failures relating to allegedly known cybersecurity =
risks and vulnerabilities.=E2=80=9D SolarWinds sells network management sof=
tware to companies and governments, including =E2=80=9Can information techn=
ology infrastructure and management platform=E2=80=9D called Orion. In 2020=
, Orion was <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/332087=
05.258413/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZ2FvLmdvdi9ibG9nL3NvbGFyd2luZHMtY3liZXJhdHRhY2stZ=
GVtYW5kcy1zaWduaWZpY2FudC1mZWRlcmFsLWFuZC1wcml2YXRlLXNlY3Rvci1yZXNwb25zZS1p=
bmZvZ3JhcGhpYw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBde6c830d" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" it=
emscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decor=
ation: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000=
000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> famously hacked =
by Russian state actors</a>, who inserted hidden code into Orion software u=
pdates and were =E2=80=9Cthen able to remotely exploit the networks and sys=
tems of SolarWinds=E2=80=99 customers,=E2=80=9D which they used =E2=80=9Cfo=
r the primary purpose of espionage.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;=
">That was bad for, among other things, SolarWinds=E2=80=99 stock price. Th=
e <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/33208705.258413/=
aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9maWxlcy9saXRpZ2F0aW9uL2NvbXBsYWludHMvMjAyMy9jb21=
wLXByMjAyMy0yMjcucGRm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB8fd689e5" itemprop=3D"StoryL=
ink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; tex=
t-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-colo=
r: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> SEC=E2=80=
=99s complaint</a> says:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On Dec=
ember 14, 2020, the day it filed the Form 8-K first announcing the SUNBURST=
 attack against the Orion platform, SolarWinds=E2=80=99 stock price dropped=
 more than 16%. It dropped at least an additional 8% the next day. The stoc=
k price continued to drop and lost approximately 35% of its value by the en=
d of the month as SolarWinds disclosed more details of the SUNBURST attack,=
 and as news outlets reported that internal sources had warned SolarWinds f=
or several years about the Company=E2=80=99s cybersecurity risks and vulner=
abilities.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">My non-technical th=
eory is that =E2=80=9C<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/cl=
ick/33208705.258413/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGV=
zLzIwMjEtMDItMDMvZ29sZG1hbi1zYWNocy1nb2VzLXRvLXN1cHJlbWUtY291cnQtaGVkZ2UtZn=
VuZHMtd29uLW9uLWdhbWVzdG9wLWtrcG9lNndzP2NtcGlkPUJCRDEwMzEyM19NT05FWVNUVUZGJ=
nV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIzMTAzMSZ1=
dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBad22d59f" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
everything is securities fraud</a>=E2=80=9D: If a public company does a bad=
 thing, or a bad thing happens to it, and the stock drops, then that is sec=
urities fraud. The stock=C2=A0<em>was=C2=A0</em>high, because investors did=
 not know about the company=E2=80=99s vulnerability to the bad thing. Then =
the bad thing happened, investors found out, and the stock dropped. Before =
they found out, they were deceived about the true value of the company, so =
that was fraud. Here, that is all you really need to know: bad thing, stock=
 down, securities fraud.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But that is not te=
chnically an accurate description of the law, so the SEC, in suing SolarWin=
ds, needs to argue that SolarWinds made false statements about facts that w=
ere material to investors. Thus, for example, =E2=80=9Cpassword=E2=80=9D:</=
p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">SolarWinds=E2=80=99 Security Sta=
tement falsely claimed the Company not only had, but enforced, a strong pas=
sword policy. Specifically, SolarWinds and Brown stated: =E2=80=9CWe requir=
e that authorized users be provisioned with unique account IDs. Our passwor=
d policy covers all applicable information systems, applications, and datab=
ases. Our password best practices enforce the use of complex passwords that=
 include both alpha and numeric characters, which are deployed to protect a=
gainst unauthorized use of passwords.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"mar=
gin: 16px 0;">Contrary to its Security Statement, SolarWinds did not enforc=
e strong password requirements on all of its information systems, applicati=
ons, and databases, as Brown and SolarWinds knew or were reckless or neglig=
ent in not knowing. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In an April 2=
017 email to the newly hired CIO, a SolarWinds employee expressed surprise =
that things =E2=80=9Clike =E2=80=98default passwords=E2=80=99 are [still] p=
laguing us when the product has been in the market [this long,]=E2=80=9D ex=
plaining, =E2=80=9C[m]any of these vulnerabilities seem pretty well amateur=
 hour.=E2=80=9D As an example, the employee noted one product for which the=
 default password was =E2=80=9Cpassword.=E2=80=9D Senior InfoSec Manager E =
testified that having a default password of =E2=80=9Cpassword=E2=80=9D is a=
 =E2=80=9Cpoor security practice.=E2=80=9D</p></blockquote><p style=3D"marg=
in: 16px 0;">And possibly even better, =E2=80=9Csolarwinds123=E2=80=9D:</p>=
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">During the Relevant Period, SolarW=
inds used an Akamai server to distribute software updates to its customers.=
 In November 2019, an outside security researcher notified SolarWinds that =
the password for the Company=E2=80=99s Akamai server was publicly available=
, and that a threat actor could use that public password to infect SolarWin=
ds=E2=80=99 software updates: =E2=80=9CI have found a public Github repo wh=
ich is leaking ftp credential belong[ing] to SolarWinds=E2=80=A6. Via this =
any hacker could upload malicious exe [executable code] and update it with =
release [of] SolarWinds product.=E2=80=9D Senior InfoSec Manager E confirme=
d the security researcher=E2=80=99s description. The password that was publ=
icly available was =E2=80=9Csolarwinds123,=E2=80=9D an astonishingly simple=
 password that did not comply with the Company=E2=80=99s stated password co=
mplexity requirements.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=
=9CAny hacker could upload malicious exe=E2=80=9D is pretty much what ultim=
ately happened and crashed the stock. I am not a cybersecurity expert and I=
 have not followed the SolarWinds hack closely, but I suppose it is possibl=
e that Russian intelligence agents were able to hack the Orion updates, and=
 thus spy on US federal government computers, by correctly guessing the pas=
sword =E2=80=9Csolarwinds123.=E2=80=9D What would <a href=3D"https://link.m=
ail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/33208705.258413/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuaW1kYi5jb20=
vdGl0bGUvdHQwMDk0MDEyL2NoYXJhY3RlcnMvbm0wMDAxNTQ4/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB=
da6aaabb" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" =
style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wi=
dth: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgro=
und-color: #ccc;"> Dark Helmet</a> say?</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But=
 that=E2=80=99s not the point; the point is that SolarWinds=C2=A0<em>invest=
ors=C2=A0</em>were, let us just hypothesize, closely reading the company=E2=
=80=99s =E2=80=9CSecurity Statement,=E2=80=9D a document that was not actua=
lly included in SolarWinds=E2=80=99 securities filings or financial reports=
 but that was =E2=80=9Cposted to the Company=E2=80=99s website.=E2=80=9D An=
d, says the SEC, they were deceived:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16p=
x 0;">SolarWinds and Brown=E2=80=99s misstatements and omissions regarding =
password issues were not only false and misleading, but materially so. A re=
asonable investor, considering whether to purchase or sell SolarWinds stock=
, would have considered it important to know the true state of SolarWinds=
=E2=80=99 password policies, especially considering that these issues were =
longstanding and potentially affected customer-facing areas such as the Aka=
mai server used to send updates to customers.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"m=
argin: 16px 0;">Is that right? It feels not quite right, in the sense that =
you rarely see equity research notes about public companies that are like =
=E2=80=9Cupgrading this company to a Buy based on its strong password polic=
ies.=E2=80=9D The claim here is not=C2=A0<em>really</em>, not=C2=A0<em>seri=
ously</em>, that investors read SolarWinds=E2=80=99=C2=A0password policy, a=
nd decided to invest based on that policy, and then lost money when the pas=
sword policy turned out to be fake. The more likely story is that investors=
 blithely assumed most companies have good practices across a range of doma=
ins and figured that, if SolarWinds really=C2=A0<em>was=C2=A0</em>just lett=
ing anyone into its software, someone would tell them.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D=
"margin: 16px 0;">It=E2=80=99s not just passwords, to be clear. The SEC ide=
ntifies other kinds of poor security information security practices; the =
=E2=80=9Cpassword=E2=80=9D stuff is just the most obvious. The basic idea i=
s that SolarWinds did various careless things, while telling customers and =
investors that it was careful, and eventually the carelessness caught up to=
 it. And the stock dropped.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">What is the les=
son that companies should take from this case? The SEC complaint says that,=
 even as SolarWinds was publicly saying that it had a good security policy,=
 =E2=80=9CBrown and other SolarWinds employees knew that SolarWinds had ser=
ious cybersecurity deficiencies.=E2=80=9D It cites a bunch of internal emai=
ls and messages:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">An August 2019=
 presentation warned that =E2=80=9C[a]ccess and privilege to critical syste=
ms / data is inappropriate.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Presen=
tations in March and October 2020 highlighted =E2=80=9C[s]ignificant defici=
encies=E2=80=9D in SolarWinds=E2=80=99 access controls. =E2=80=A6</p><p sty=
le=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In a July 2020 email to Brown, a member of the Engin=
eering team described being =E2=80=9Cspooked=E2=80=9D by activity at a Sola=
rWinds=E2=80=99 customer. Brown agreed that the incident was =E2=80=9C<stro=
ng>very concerning</strong>=E2=80=9D and continued, =E2=80=9CAs you guys kn=
ow <strong>our backends are not that resilient</strong> and we should defin=
itely make them better.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A Septembe=
r 2020 Risk Acceptance Form flagged for Brown and others =E2=80=9Cthe risk =
of legacy issues in the Orion Platform=E2=80=9D and warned =E2=80=9C[t]he v=
olume of security issues being identified over the last month have <strong>=
outstripped the capacity</strong> of Engineering teams to resolve.=E2=80=9D=
</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In instant messages sent in November 2020,=
 SolarWinds=E2=80=99 Senior InfoSec Manager E expressed his own disgust wit=
h the Company=E2=80=99s security posture, lamenting, =E2=80=9C<strong>[W]e=
=E2=80=99re so far from being a security minded company.</strong> [E]very t=
ime I hear about our head geeks talking about security I want to throw up.=
=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In November 2020, a SolarWinds In=
formation Security employee sent an instant message to Senior InfoSec Manag=
er E with a link to a list of vulnerabilities in the Orion platform stating=
, <strong>=E2=80=9CThe products are riddled and obviously have been for man=
y years.=E2=80=9D</strong></p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If =
you run information security at a software company, would you rather get me=
ssages like this, or not? Like:</p><ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; p=
adding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Id=
eally your security would be perfect and you would never get messages like =
this because there are no problems.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;=
">If your security is <em>not </em>perfect, wouldn=E2=80=99t you=C2=A0rathe=
r get messages like this than not?? For one thing, strong clear intemperate=
 statements like this will alert you to the problem better than diplomatic =
wishy-washy messages. For another thing, you might want to employ engineers=
 who <em>send </em>messages like this: You want your employees to be morall=
y indignant at security vulnerabilities, to get mad about them, so they are=
 likely to catch and fix them. You want people who are passionate about sec=
urity, and if you find them you will tolerate their negativity and complain=
ing.</li></ol><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But if you run the legal departm=
ent at a software company, these messages are really bad! They lead pretty =
directly to securities fraud charges. I <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomb=
ergbusiness.com/click/33208705.258413/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vc=
GluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjAtMDEtMTAva2VlcC10aGUtY2xvd25zLW91dC1vZi1lbWFpbD9j=
bXBpZD1CQkQxMDMxMjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV=
3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzEwMzEmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef=
4b8524a319a23cB723ed6dc" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" tar=
get=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; b=
order-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style:=
 solid; background-color: #ccc;">  once wrote</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you are trying to build a good engineering culture,=
 you might want to encourage your employees to send hyperbolic, overstated,=
 highly quotable emails to a broad internal distribution list when they obj=
ect to a decision. On the other hand your lawyers, and your public relation=
s people, will obviously and correctly tell you that that is insane.</p></b=
lockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Everything is securities fraud, and =
using =E2=80=9Cpassword=E2=80=9D as your password is securities fraud, but =
using =E2=80=9Cpassword=E2=80=9D as your password is especially securities =
fraud if one of your engineers sends an email saying that it is =E2=80=9Cpr=
etty well amateur hour.=E2=80=9D But it is pretty well amateur hour! It is =
helpful for someone to point that out. But it gets you sued.</p><table clas=
s=3D"liveintent-sponsored-content" width=3D"auto" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspa=
cing=3D"0">
<tr>
<td colspan=3D"2" align=3D"left" valign=3D"top" style=3D"padding:0 0px">=20
<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/33208705.258413/aH=

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">When did the shares of Bed Bath & =

December 2022, hired financial advisers to find a potential buyer (in bankr=

 but not enough to pay them in full, and certainly not with anything left o=
ver for shareholders.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But Bed Bath & Be=
yond found a way to delay the inevitable: It had enthusiastic retail meme-s=

nd over to its creditors. This was pretty clearly the plan, and it was pret=

about what it was doing. But it did it anyway, and the shareholders happily=
 tricked themselves, pouring money into a worthless company for it to hand =

rs wanted to get their money back, and realized that there was a trade to b=
e done that would make everyone, temporarily, happy. So it did the trade.</=
